When trying to maintain balance for a long period of time you need to try to avoid having to make large adjustments by making many small adjustments before things get out of hand.
With large adjustments it is easier to overreact and fall

I am currently experimenting a lot with embodiment and I can totally see how that principle applies to thinking and being in general.
I am interested to see if it also becomes easier by improving my physical sense of balance.

I just sat on a log that was over the water and the threat of falling in made my senses much sharper.
Even if it wasn't a big threat.

This seems like an interesting concept to play around with.
How can you create mildly threatening environments that are actually harmless?

Trying to seem like someone is much harder than actually becoming that person

Within limits of course 😄

After watching a video by @Malcolm_Ocean on the importance of finding your zone of proximal development, I've had some important insights on how I can feel strong within "weakness"

(1/3)

Now I try to:
- listen to my body-mind
- try to not exceed my zone of proximal development unless necessary
- push myself when I feel ready
- balance rest and growth
- lower my expectations for myself. This has to be embodied and can't just be an intellectual decision 

(2/3)

I feel that showing this combination of fierce/tender self-compassion and empathy for myself heals the self inflicted dualism of mind and body.
They learn to trust each other again and become one unit.

(3/3)

It wasn't quite this extreme but I used to basically only allow myself to feel emotions I could label. One reason that this has been an unhelpful approach is that feelings are like color:🧵

There probably is a set of primitive feelings that are easy to name but in combination, they can create an infinite number of complex experiences that have never been seen before. Giving a well-fitting name to every color combination is both impossible and unnecessary

If I frantically try to find a good name for my current experience I tend to forget to actually experience it. Being able to untangle the different primitive feelings and their possible effect on the resulting experience is very useful but only if it isn't overdone

Striking that balance is very difficult but I like this quote from Alan Watts: "Thoughts are a good servant but a bad master". As a rule of thumb I try to be aware when I am being controlled by my thoughts instead of living the experience

Define work as putting your attention and your hands on the tools of your craft for pre-defined and uninterrupted periods of time. Everytime your attention moves away from the tools, you compassionately but firmly place it back. Quantity and quality of output are irrelevant. 🧵

If you just sat there doing nothing but keeping your attention on your tools, you achieved 100% of what you needed to achieve. In actuality this is really unlikely to happen. Your brain hates boredom and will just start working once your attention settles in.
And you can worry about the quality later. In the moment of creation it just takes attention away from the doing.

Be generous with what you consider a tool but try to not deceive yourself. If you are a writer, keyboard and writing app are your primary tools but perhaps you also spend time in your thoughts and imagination or you use the internet for research.
It is easy to engage in seemingly productive procrastination with those last three but if you are aware and honest with yourself you usually know that you are doing it.
With this method I find that "ugh fields" tend to dissolve or I suddenly understand what caused them. Then I can work towards resolving the cause.
I try to make this method non-coercive so make more use of fierce self-compassion and expanded concentration rather than force.

@the_wilderless I have been thinking about our conversation of taming the optimizing aspects of the intellect. One thing that has helped me is turning the intellect in on itself. It craves perfection but its optimization efforts are often destructive and therefore imperfect

This means an intellect that "can't behave itself" necessarily creates the conditions it wants to avoid. I feel like my intellect has understood this somewhat and learned to temper itself. It's actually been grateful for the break😄

I am curious if this resonates with you at all

Or I may be living inside of a horror story without realizing it 😄 What I think I mean is making the intellect aware of itself *as an intellect* by extending its models about itself, its relationships and its objectives. 🧵

https://t.co/BHORGL0kjf

It can then reason about itself as part of a greater whole that it wants to help instead of assuming it *is* the whole. This means it can interrupt itself without needing some other part to step in. Or it just means that it is ok with being interrupted and doesn't resist

For example, I may be starting to go down a destructive intellectual rabbit hole. The intellect is using its models and conceptualizations for this. But then, inside of those models, there is the understanding that all of this "optimizing" isn't actually gonna go anywhere helpful
It's not going to help it achieve its objectives of helping my whole being suceed. So it is comfortable abandoning, pausing or redirecting all of this unskilfull intellectualizing.

The intellect can only work in terms of models and concepts. It is by design not embodied. So it makes sense to me that integrating it in a healthy way requires that it becomes self-aware of its place in the whole being through models and reason. That is the only way it can act

I did once have a strong somatic experience of my intellect "breaking off" from the whole. I felt I could see how all of my rational thoughts were coming from it and that its access to the rest of my awareness is limited.

This strong sense of separation didn't last but I feel my  rational side has become more humble and less internally aggressive since then.

I hope this makes it more clear what I am trying to point at 😄

The fundamental mythological struggle I find myself in is whether a life guided purely by joy and aliveness, rather than self-coercion, is viable or just a pipe dream. 
Mind you I don't expect it to *consist* purely of joy and aliveness, merely that it is guided by them.

It seems there is conflict with a perverted version of primal necessity. A part of me is saying that I ought to respect what is and must arrange myself with it to survive. Otherwise I am a naive and fanciful fool!

https://t.co/sTB5UfYY1W

There is conflict here because I can see where it's coming from. If the tribe *needs* food it doesn't really matter, whether hunting gives me joy right now. This part is very concerned with safety.
The problem comes with how perverted "necessity" has become.
Most of modern "necessity" is entirely constructed and arbitrary. It is far removed from the primal necessity to survive but modern mythology equates the two.

So what is the other side of the conflict? It is the part of me that recognizes this emptiness and how much more real value I could bring if I were to transcend it. 
Not just in theory but in practice.

This part feels deeply constrained by the arbitrary conditions placed on its existence. It is very concerned with flourishing.
I'm sure it *would* feel joy and aliveness if it could see that something was *actually* necessary.

I believe the path forward is for these two parts (and all the other ones) to collaborate on redefining necessity and navigating this new reality. What do we actually *need* to feel safe? How can we make as much space for flourishing without neglecting safety?

This will require both parts to face their fears and give each other space. Coercion and force have no place in this process. I must find ways to meditate between the two and make both of them feel seen and heard. They are both valuable.

Realized I don't feel safe to follow my own judgement but comfortable that I can handle whatever life throws at me

Like the thought of quitting scares me but the thought of getting fired does not (or rather my agency feels bigger than my fear)

That feels unintegrated to me

Actually "I don't feel safe to trust my own judgement" really doesn't feel true at all

It is more that there is a mismatch between the things I feel I can handle if I decide to do them vs. if they are forced on me by circumstances

As if the act of actively deciding meant I suddenly couldn't do it anymore

And somehow that feels true?!

Actively deciding ups the internal pressure and perfectionism. Suddenly it has to be great

Whereas if it is forced upon me by circumstances "good enough" will do

Also circumstances can be chaotic or destructive but they can't be delusional. They have their own incorruptible pacing and logic

Whereas my decisions or potential decisions often caused me great harm through internal violence in the past

This doubt has the trappings of a self-fulfilling prophecy. Acts of god don't contain this doubt

So scenarios in which I have made an active decision are more difficult to handle partly *because* of this doubt

Very very interesting 🤔

It's become a common observation that we have few real elders. However, a much more devastating critique is that the modern world has very few true adults. Most of us are trapped in an incomplete adolescence with no one to guide us to adulthood. (not a direct quote)

An adult being "someone who knows why they were born, knows who they are as a unique participant in the web of life and who creatively occupies their distinctive eco-niche in their everyday life as a gift to their people and the greater Earth community"
I've felt this for a long time and it's always been extremely frustrating that everyone seemed like they were pretending to be mature adults. I knew I wasn't either but at least I could tell.
Having no one to guide me in a way that actually mattered felt very isolating and lonely. It also made me very skeptical and careful about what I consume and integrate into myself.

Seeing this articulated in such a compelling way makes me feel seen. "Yes thank you for pointing out there are no adults around, we are all super immature, hurting each other and pretending it has to be this way"

I feel like in many ways tpot has become my emergent Elder. Not an individual that can guide me through my development into true adulthood but a patchwork of adults and mature adolescents, each carrying a piece of the puzzle.
https://t.co/q6nQMfPQPq

"the vital need to restore or enhance the process of human development" as an answer to our crises, in particular the ecological crisis.

Also makes me feel seen. The focus on symptoms frustrated me for a long time as it seemed clear that there is more fundamental work to do

This is not to say that symptomatic work is bad. What frustrated me was that I couldn't see anyone that understood the need for a fundamental renewal of culture in a way that made sense and wasn't just another symptomatic remedy in disguise

Of course I didn't have nearly the same words, as I do now or as Plotkin does but this has been cooking in me for 4 years or so. Feels good to find something that connects with that original spark in such an articulate way
https://t.co/q6nQMfPQPq

Minor point but Plotkin talks about true Adulthood enabling the possibility for extraordinary lives and lists a bunch of influential people. "possibility" hints at this but he doesn't say that you can be an adult without being extraordinary on a globally recognizable scale.
I'm fairly confident he would agree but serving your local community in this way seems more than enough to me. If this message reaches more people that feels important to make explicitly clear
https://t.co/q6nQMfPQPq
"The rite of passage does not bring about the passage. [..] The developmental successes throughout the previous life stage are what gives rise to the passage"
This feels important because it seems like a mistake our culture is likely to make. We love trying to skip the hard work and we habitually mistake the symbol for the thing itself
https://t.co/q6nQMfPQPq

My biggest friction point so far is Plotkin's ecological definition of soul. "I believe unique eco-niche is the best definition for soul. [..] By human Soul, I mean a person's unique place, not in human culture, but in the greather Earth community, the more-than-human world"

I see that it's intended to recreate a missing link between humans and nature and that feels powerful and necessary. I also think part of my resistance comes from a general resistance to ecological discourse that is in my shadow.

However, I can't shake the feeling that this definition is too narrow to be truly general purpose. Like it is too specifically crafted for our current crisis. This is not necessarily a bad thing but it's something I got stuck on for a bit.

All of creation is more than Earth's ecosystem and I'm missing the divine spark in this definition. I can imagine Plotkin doesn't conceive of it this way but I also can't shake the mechanistic connotations "ecosystem" has for me
https://t.co/q6nQMfPQPq

If surrender sets us free why is it so difficult? Because accepting that "this is it" is deeply terrifying and the illusion of control saves us from this pain. Even if it creates its own pain in the process

By leaning into wonder we can turn
This is it 😰 into
What is this? 😲

Example: I'm exhausted today and feel like I live in a small world. It hurts to feel the contrast to how big and joyful I know my world can be. Especially because I don't see myself trying to change it. Some loud parts really just want me to tune out and do nothing

But what if I explore this "small" reality curiously without needing it to change? What can I do? What are the consequences of doing certain activities? How can I enjoy myself? What is beautiful about this reality? What does it have to teach me?

What happens if I write a tweet about this? What happens if I really work on getting the message right instead of hitting send on the first draft? Will that be enjoyable? Or will I feel drained afterwards?

Of course I have plenty of stories about these questions but the truth is I simply don't know. And accepting that I don't know sets me free. I can just start writing the tweet and see how it goes. I can explore my reality instead of thinking I've already got it figured out

And turns out it actually was more enjoyable and energizing than the stories had me believe! I still feel exhausted and I still wanna lie down but this gentle exploration made reality feel just a little bit bigger and richer than before 😊

@yellowrainmac Regarding my own journey it feels crucial to not make the same mistake I've already made again, which is trying to skip crucial steps. Looking at what it means to be an elder when I haven't gotten past adolescence yet is tricksy.

@yellowrainmac What seems like "failures" of adolescence are actually features. Things like hubris for example. We villify it because "you are supposed to be mature" but going through a phase of hubris is crucial for development

If our biological age and developmental stage diverge it can become very difficult to progress

Many demonized aspects of adolescence (the hubris, the naivety and the recklessness for example) are not bugs they're features of finding your path.
Skipping them is catastrophic and going back as a biological adult can feel embarrassing

A plain white room. In the middle colorful strings are spanned from floor to ceiling in a random pattern. I am surprised by how much joy such a simple installation brings me. It is something whimsical and playful that I've been sorely missing from this place of seriousness

Now that I've found a new friend I want do dance with it, frolick around it and weave through its gaps. But all I feel able to permit myself in this suffocating atmosphere is a slight smile and a quickening of step. A shame really

Each moment can give rise to an infinite multitude of subjective realities. All of them valid and none of them better or worse than any other in a divine sense. They may be more or less aligned with the underlying reality and they may be more or less helpful for some goal

But they're all valid, so how do I choose which one to fall into? Mostly that's the wrong question. "I" don't. The role of the I, or ego, is to put up guard rails. To push back against realities that it perceives as harmful.

It can do its job best when it is healed and fully in service to our unique expression of the divine. The ego is illusory but it is crucial to fully manifest our unique expression of the divine in the world. I see this as the purpose of life but mostly for aesthetic reasons
So how can the ego put up guard rails? According to which values? And why are these still the wrong questions to ask? What is choosing from that infinite multitude of realities and on what basis?

The path unfolds on its own, completely without "my" involvement. And yet "I" am an intricate part in that unfolding. Illusory or not. To live as if spirit is more important than the world is just as misguided as living as if the world is more important than spirit

For the world is ultimately contained within and in service to spirit. Anything that doesn't fully recognize this is subtle spiritual bypassing. A more true goal in my eyes would be to practice maximum integration between the two domains

To create a worldly realm that is completely in service to spirit but also recognized for its essential contribution. To create a very powerful world ego, which we have done, and then point it back to complete service to the spirit realm 

What would such a world look like?

I don't need to try fixate meaning. I can stop trying to turn meaning into something stable, objective and eternal. I can stop looking for that kind of meaning
Until very recently I would have a habit of trying that constantly at a very low level. The problem is that meaning is nebulous and uncertain and squeezing it too hard crushes it

And it is tragically funny to think back to my past selves, so desperate for freely flowing meaning, and see how I was always participating in its destruction. Like a cartoon character where every episode they get what they want and then they fumble it by doing something silly
And like the cartoon character I was trapped in the logic of the medium. Every episode I would come back and do the same thing again completely unaware that I was doing it
I would find meaning placed in my hand like a delicate sand sculpture and when I inevitiably tried to hold onto it, it would run like sand through my fingers
Sometimes I would find the move, or part of the move, to not do that but then at the end of the episode I would try to hold onto that which once again crushed the meaning

I've recently discovered that, at a very low level, I was habitually crushing meaning by trying to hold onto it and I've come to understand that I don't need to do that. I'm curious to see how stable this insight will be but for now it keeps coming back https://t.co/GNCJG25vMz

So I've decided that I want to master a medium of creative expression and enjoy it! I think until recently I was still hoping for the universe to drop an objectively correct choice in my lap but in absence of that, writing seems like the one that fits me most
For now, learning to just enjoy daily creative writing by getting into the right mood for creative flow seems challenging enough, so I won't worry about publishing just yet

My biggest challenges for publishing are contuinity (picking up and connecting to an idea again), editing & constraining (facing the limitations and imperfections of an idea), and the fear of publishing itself (my ideas being misunderstood or disrespected, even by accident)

The last one won't be an issue once I have something finished in my hands, it's more a fear I have to face during the polishing process. I do have a certain hunger to master these things so I will keep an eye out for the point when just enjoying writing becomes a bit boring

Science is a very destructive observer. It tries to find the most context invariant meaning and therefore misses the biggest chunk of it 

Figuring out how we can engage with nebulous meaning rigorously is going to be a major challenge of the coming decades

Knowing that life calls me to something is not the same as giving it a full bodied yes. That is a process that needs to unfold naturally and I don't want to let myself be coerced anymore by knowing what the right decision is. My work is to find that full bodied yes

For example, I want to run a tpot summer camp this year. This feels obvious. It's the right thing to do, it aligns with what calls me and saying no would feel like a small death. But there are also parts that are afraid, uncertain or don't understand why this is obvious

The path forward is getting all of me to the table and hearing every parts voice. Connect the parts that clearly understand why this is so important with the parts that are afraid. And let the unsafe parts know that I *can* decide against life if that is the best option

An organism with less trauma outcompetes a traumatized organism. I want to hook into the existing infrastructure of competing multi-person organisms (businesses) to support propagation of healing/alignment through the system. https://t.co/XUCU43dts5

By "exploiting" (lol) existing competition environments the new paradigm gets both developed and validated at the same time. If people feel better and the system has more transpersonal power, it's better

This journey may look less competitive initially. As you move away from optimizing proxy metrics to optimizing "divine alignment" your actions will look non-sensical to agents zeroed in on the proxy. What they can't see is that you're moving out of a local optimum
As the organism continues on that journey it will soon start to outcompete its peers that are not on this path. Islands of coherence is a big factor of this

Reality is fractal and organisms will go through metaphorically comparable developmental process on all levels (dark night, trauma healing etc.) And as higher level organisms move towards "divine alignment" their member organisms are called to do the same and vice versa
The higher level organism acts as a coherence anchor. It creates problems for the component organisms to grow around

There is a theory of trauma here that draws a lot on the symmetry theory of valence. "Divine alignment" is the absence of suffering which is the absence of tension. This is a direction not an end goal

"The divine as a coordination mechanism"
Right and left hemisphere and the fall from Eden. Harmonizing divine and worldly realm and putting the now ridiculously more powerful worldly realm completely in service of the divine

But none of this is supernatural in the way that we currently think of the religious. It just turns out that natural reality is a lot more magical than we thought for a while. All of this craziness adds up to normality at the end

This is mirrored on the dividual path to enlightenment (divine alignment). For a while it gets super crazy and explosive but as you get used to that aspect of reality it calms down. It can even feel boring (I remember @aleksil79 tweeting about this at some point)
